summary:
Shulaveris Gora is a Neolithic settlement mound in the Kvemo Kartli region of modern Georgia, associated with the broader Shulaveri-Shomu culture that flourished in the South Caucasus from approximately 6000–4000 BCE. The site preserves evidence of circular mudbrick architecture, domesticated animals including cattle and pigs, and cultivated grains — marking it as one of the earliest known agricultural settlements in the Caucasus region.

The Shulaveri-Shomu culture is significant because it represents the spread of the Neolithic package — domesticated crops and animals, permanent settlement, pottery — into the mountainous Caucasus corridor between Anatolia and Central Asia. The region would later play an important role in the diffusion of both agricultural and metallurgical innovations between the Near East and the Eurasian steppe. Shulaveris Gora is also notable for evidence of early viticulture, placing the South Caucasus among the candidate regions for the independent origins of wine production.
